title: Hip Fracture in 84-Year-Old with Postoperative Delirium and Polypharmacy Cascade
subtitle: The Lorazepam Caused the Fall, Surgery Within 48 Hours Saves Lives, Fascia Iliaca Block + Scheduled Tylenol + Avoid Beers Meds — and Mobilization on POD 1 Beats Bed Rest

patient.name: E. Lindqvist (Eleanor, 84 F)
patient.code: Full Code (advance directive: full code with reassessment if poor functional prognosis; daughter is POA)
patient.admitDate: Yesterday, 2230 (ED arrival via EMS after found on floor by daughter who came over after Eleanor missed evening phone call)
patient.room: Ortho post-op Unit, Bed 7 (POD 1; door-side observation given delirium risk)

vitals.time: Today 0900 (CURRENT — POD 1, 6 hr after fascia iliaca block + cephalomedullary nail fixation done last evening)
vitals.hr: 92 bpm (intermittent atrial flutter on tele — known paroxysmal AFib)
vitals.temp: 99.4°F (low-grade — common POD 1, atelectasis or expected inflammation)

labs.v: Found on floor of bedroom by daughter at 2200; no witnessed fall; patient unable to recall the fall; last seen well at 1900 on the evening phone call; new symptom in recent 2 weeks = 'anxiety about being alone' for which PCP prescribed LORAZEPAM 0.5 mg PO BID 10 days ago; took last lorazepam dose at 1700 (5 hr before fall); also takes 8 other medications (polypharmacy)
labs.r: Mechanical fall in elderly is rarely 'just a fall' — must identify the precipitant (new medication, syncope, infection, arrhythmia, environmental, neurologic). Here: LORAZEPAM is a BEERS LIST medication absolutely contraindicated in elderly for anxiety; major contributor to falls + delirium. Should never have been prescribed. Cascade: anxiety symptom → lorazepam → fall → hip fracture

labs.v: X-ray and CT: displaced INTERTROCHANTERIC FEMUR FRACTURE right hip (extracapsular); no other fractures (head, c-spine, ribs, pelvis cleared); ORIF with CEPHALOMEDULLARY NAIL last evening (yesterday 1830–2030) — within 24 hr of admission per geriatric hip fracture protocol; FASCIA ILIACA BLOCK placed by anesthesia before incision (opioid-sparing); allowed WEIGHT-BEARING AS TOLERATED postop
labs.r: Hip fractures in elderly: 25% ONE-YEAR MORTALITY; 50% of survivors do not return to prior function; SURGERY WITHIN 24–48 HR reduces mortality, pneumonia, delirium, DVT, decubitus injury vs delayed surgery. Intertrochanteric (extracapsular) fractures usually fixed with nail or DHS; femoral neck (intracapsular) often replaced (hemiarthroplasty or THA) due to AVN risk. WBAT post-op standard now (was 6 weeks NWB historically — outdated; immobility worsens outcomes more than micro-displacement risk)

labs.v: Pre-fall baseline (per daughter): mild cognitive impairment (MMSE 26 last year, 24 last month — declining), oriented x3 most days, lives alone with daily check-ins from daughter and weekly meal prep. POSTOPERATIVELY: CAM POSITIVE at 0700 today — (1) acute change from baseline + fluctuating ✓, (2) inattention (cannot count backward from 10) ✓, (3) disorganized thinking (rambling, not following questions logically) ✓ — POSTOP DELIRIUM diagnosed Grade 1 (mild hyperactive delirium)
labs.r: Postop delirium incidence in elderly hip fracture patients = 25–50%; risk factors here: age >80 ✓, baseline cognitive impairment ✓, polypharmacy ✓, recent Beers-list medication (lorazepam!) ✓, surgery ✓, opioid exposure ✓, hypoxia (SpO2 94%) ✓. CAM is the standard screening tool (4-item: acute/fluctuating + inattention + either disorganized thinking or altered LOC). Hyperactive delirium = agitated, restless; hypoactive delirium = quiet, withdrawn (MORE COMMON and MORE MISSED; higher mortality)

labs.n: HOME MEDICATIONS — POLYPHARMACY review (9 meds, multiple Beers-list)
labs.v: 1) LORAZEPAM 0.5 mg PO BID — started 10 days ago for anxiety (BEERS — cause of fall, MUST DC); 2) DIPHENHYDRAMINE 25 mg PO PRN sleep (BEERS — anticholinergic, fall/delirium risk, DC); 3) OXYBUTYNIN 5 mg PO BID for urge incontinence (BEERS — anticholinergic, delirium risk, DC + consider alternative like behavioral interventions or mirabegron); 4) AMITRIPTYLINE 25 mg PO QHS for neuropathic pain (BEERS — anticholinergic, sedating, fall/delirium risk, DC + alternative like gabapentin at low dose or topical lidocaine); 5) levothyroxine 50 mcg daily (continue); 6) amlodipine 5 mg daily (continue, monitor BP); 7) atorvastatin 20 mg daily (continue); 8) vitamin D 1000 IU daily (continue); 9) calcium 500 mg TID (continue for osteoporosis)
labs.r: FOUR BEERS-LIST medications — each independently raises fall + delirium risk; together they form a 'polypharmacy cascade.' Pharmacy review + deprescribing is the single highest-impact intervention. Replace with non-Beers alternatives or non-pharm approaches. Anticholinergic burden score >3 = significant delirium risk

labs.n: Pain assessment (PAINAD scale — verbal report less reliable in delirium)
labs.v: PAINAD score 4/10 at rest (mild grimacing, occasional moaning); 7/10 with movement (severe grimacing, body rigidity, calling out 'hurts'); fascia iliaca block placed preop is wearing off (6-hr duration of bupivacaine block); scheduled acetaminophen + low-dose hydromorphone PRN ordered
labs.r: PAINAD (Pain Assessment in Advanced Dementia) scale 0–10 assesses breathing, vocalization, facial expression, body language, consolability; designed for nonverbal/confused patients. Untreated pain is a major delirium PRECIPITANT — adequate pain control is essential. Goal: scheduled non-opioid (acetaminophen) + judicious PRN opioid (NOT MEPERIDINE which is BEERS); AVOID anticholinergics, benzos. Consider fascia iliaca block re-dosing or continuous infusion catheter

labs.v: Preop ECG: paroxysmal atrial flutter Day of admission (rate 110 → 88 with metoprolol 5 mg IV); patient has known paroxysmal AFib on warfarin (INR 2.4 on admission — held before surgery, given 4 PCC for partial reversal, warfarin restarted POD 1); Echo (last year): EF 55%, mild MR; cardiology consulted preop, cleared for surgery with continued metoprolol
labs.r: Cardiac assessment crucial preop in elderly. Anticoagulation reversal balance: hold warfarin or use 4PCC; restart postop once hemostasis confirmed; bridge with prophylactic doses. AFib on warfarin: high stroke risk in elderly off anticoagulant — restart as soon as safe (typically POD 1–3). DVT prophylaxis for hip fracture is mandatory (high VTE risk)

labs.v: Preop: Hgb 11.2, Plt 188, INR 2.4 (off warfarin x 2 doses, partial reversal with 4PCC), BMP normal, Cr 1.0 (eGFR 58 — mild CKD age-related), albumin 3.2 (low — frailty marker). POD 1: Hgb 8.6 (drop from 11.2 = ~2.6 g/dL loss — combination of surgical loss + dilutional + chronic anemia of frailty); Plt 168; INR 1.4 (post-PCC + held warfarin); BMP normal; lactate 1.4 (no shock); CRP elevated (expected postop)
labs.r: Postop Hgb drop expected from blood loss + dilution + suppressed erythropoiesis; TRANSFUSION THRESHOLD in elderly cardiac patients = Hgb <8 (FOCUS trial). She's at 8.6 — borderline; trend; transfuse if drops <8 OR symptomatic anemia. Albumin <3.5 = poor nutritional/frailty marker — outcomes worse

labs.v: Mild dehydration on admission (skin tenting, dry mucosa, cap refill 2 sec); UOP via foley today 30 mL/hr (borderline); breakfast intake 25% (poor PO); BUN 22 (mildly elevated from dehydration); calorie/protein intake estimated 40% of needs over past week per daughter
labs.r: Elderly hip fracture patients often poorly hydrated and undernourished pre-fall; postop nutrition is critical for wound healing and recovery. Supplemental nutrition (oral nutritional supplements between meals; consider nutrition consult and adapted diet textures if swallow concern)

labs.v: Baseline 2 months ago: independent ambulating with cane at home, drove short distances, did own ADLs, MoCA 24 (mild cognitive impairment); current POD 1: NOT yet ambulated; physical therapy consulted; WEIGHT-BEARING AS TOLERATED per orthopedic surgeon; plan: out of bed to chair this AM with PT assist; first ambulation later today
labs.r: EARLY MOBILIZATION POD 1 is standard of care for hip fracture in elderly — reduces complications (DVT, pneumonia, decubitus, delirium, functional decline); WBAT for most modern fixations. The historic 'NWB x 6 weeks' is OUTDATED for the typical geriatric hip fracture; immobility kills more elderly patients than micro-implant displacement

labs.v: Foley placed preop yesterday for surgery (anesthesia request); currently to gravity; PLAN to REMOVE TODAY POD 1 per CAUTI prevention protocol; will do bladder scan to check for retention; if retention >300 mL, intermittent straight cath or recheck after fluid management
labs.r: CAUTI prevention: REMOVE FOLEY ASAP postop; ideally within 24 hr of surgery if not anesthesia-mandated. Indwelling foley = CAUTI risk + restraint-like effect + impedes mobilization. Use bladder scans, scheduled toileting, external female catheter (PureWick) if accurate output critical, intermittent catheterization for retention

labs.v: Pneumatic compression devices (SCDs) bilateral lower extremities continuously since admission; ENOXAPARIN 40 mg SubQ daily started POD 1 at 0600 (12 hr postop, hemostasis confirmed); continue for 28–35 days postop (hip fracture is highest-risk surgery for VTE); aspirin alternative for low-risk per ACCP guidelines (not applicable here given anticoagulation history)
labs.r: Hip fracture surgery = HIGHEST RISK for VTE (DVT 40–60%, PE 5–10% without prophylaxis). Pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH, fondaparinux, DOAC, warfarin) for 28–35 days post-discharge. Mechanical (SCDs) immediate continuous. Watch for bleeding signs

labs.v: 1:1 sitter at bedside; daughter spending nights at bedside; bedside large-print clock; window blinds open during day; minimize stimulation; cluster nursing care; sleep hygiene; reorientation at every interaction; glasses + hearing aids (daughter brought from home); avoid Beers meds; AVOID restraints (worsen delirium and increase falls/injury); avoid awakening 2200–0600 unless clinically necessary
labs.r: HELP (Hospital Elder Life Program) bundle — non-pharm delirium management evidence-based; reduces delirium incidence by ~30–40%. NEVER use diphenhydramine, benzodiazepines, anticholinergics for sleep or agitation in geriatric inpatient — they WORSEN delirium and falls

labs.v: Daughter (only child, RN background) is POA and healthcare proxy; advance directive 'full code with reassessment if poor functional prognosis'; daughter's goal = restore mother to baseline at home OR SNF if cannot; daughter informed about typical post-hip-fracture trajectory (25% 1-yr mortality, 50% don't return to prior function, half who do return need more support); daughter realistic and engaged
labs.r: Goals-of-care conversation early in hip fracture care: align treatment intensity with prognosis and patient values. Advance directive in chart; daughter as informed proxy; transparent prognosis communication; SNF vs home with services planning

nursesNote: 0900 — 84 y/o female POD 1 from cephalomedullary nail fixation of right intertrochanteric femur fracture (done last evening 1830–2030, within 24 hr of admission per geriatric hip fracture protocol). MECHANISM was a fall at home; she was found on the floor by her daughter at 2200 yesterday after missing her evening phone call. The root-cause investigation reveals the fall was almost certainly precipitated by LORAZEPAM 0.5 mg PO BID started by her PCP 10 days ago for new symptom of 'anxiety about being alone' — a BEERS LIST medication absolutely contraindicated in elderly. She is on EIGHT other medications including FOUR additional Beers-list items (diphenhydramine PRN sleep, oxybutynin for incontinence, amitriptyline for neuropathic pain — anticholinergic burden score >3, with diphenhydramine/lorazepam/amitriptyline being heavily sedating). Polypharmacy with multiple Beers-list meds = the geriatric 'cascade' that produced this admission. Comprehensive pharmacy review at admission and on the floor today: DISCONTINUED lorazepam, diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, amitriptyline; alternatives proposed (behavioral intervention for incontinence, gabapentin or topical lidocaine for neuropathic pain, sleep hygiene + melatonin or trazodone 12.5 mg if needed). Surgery yesterday went well: fascia iliaca block by anesthesia preop (opioid-sparing analgesia x 6 hr post-block — wearing off this AM), cephalomedullary nail fixation, estimated blood loss 250 mL, anesthesia spinal + light sedation (avoided general where possible — less delirium in elderly per recent meta-analyses). Allowed WEIGHT-BEARING AS TOLERATED postop (modern standard; outdated NWB-x-6-weeks practice no longer recommended for typical geriatric hip fracture). PHYSICAL THERAPY consulted, will see her this AM; plan: out of bed to chair this morning, ambulate with PT assist later today. POD 1 development: at 0700 routine assessment, found her CAM POSITIVE for postop delirium (acute change + inattention + disorganized thinking) — Grade 1 hyperactive (mild agitation, restless, pulling at IV lines, repeatedly asking 'where am I?'). Postop delirium incidence in elderly hip fracture = 25–50%; risk factors here all stacked (age >80, baseline mild cognitive impairment, polypharmacy with multiple Beers meds, recent benzo, surgery, opioids, mild hypoxia SpO2 94%, pain, foley, NPO, unfamiliar environment). Non-pharmacologic delirium management bundle initiated: 1:1 sitter; daughter rooming-in; bedside large-print clock + calendar; window blinds open; reorientation at every interaction; glasses + hearing aids in place (daughter brought from home — KEY intervention); minimize stimulation + clustering of care; sleep hygiene with no awakening 2200–0600 unless clinical; AVOID restraints (worsen delirium and falls); AVOID Beers meds (no benzos, no diphenhydramine, no haloperidol unless severe agitation threatening safety after non-pharm exhausted — and only at lowest dose with informed family discussion given black-box dementia mortality warning). PAIN MANAGEMENT: PAINAD 4 at rest, 7 with movement (block wearing off); scheduled ACETAMINOPHEN 650 mg PO q6h (NOT PRN); HYDROMORPHONE 0.2 mg IV q4h PRN moderate-severe pain (low dose, hydromorphone preferred over morphine in elderly because no toxic metabolite accumulation; AVOID MEPERIDINE which is Beers); discussed re-dosing or continuous fascia iliaca catheter with anesthesia. POD 1 LABS: Hgb 8.6 (down from 11.2 pre-op — expected) — borderline cardiac transfusion threshold (FOCUS trial: transfuse if <8 in cardiac patients or symptomatic); trend; INR 1.4 (warfarin held for surgery, partially reversed with 4PCC preop, restart today); BMP normal; mild hypoalbuminemia 3.2 (frailty marker; nutrition consult). VITAL SIGNS: stable, low-grade temp 99.4 (POD 1 common from atelectasis or inflammation, not necessarily infection — incentive spirometry + early mobility); SpO2 94% on 2 L NC (mild hypoxia contributing to delirium — encourage IS, mobilization, ambulation). FOLEY CATHETER to be REMOVED today per CAUTI prevention; will do bladder scan if needed. VTE PROPHYLAXIS: SCDs bilateral continuous since admission; ENOXAPARIN 40 mg SubQ daily started this AM (12 hr postop, hemostasis confirmed); continue 28–35 days postop. Daughter (POA, RN background) at bedside; goals-of-care discussion ongoing — wants mother to return to baseline at home, accepts SNF as bridge if needed; advance directive in chart 'full code with reassessment if poor functional prognosis.' Anticipated trajectory: PT/OT, mobilization, delirium resolution as precipitants addressed (typically days-weeks in elderly with baseline cognitive impairment), discharge to SNF in ~5–7 days for ongoing rehab, home with services in ~3–4 weeks if function allows. PCP notified about the lorazepam–fall connection (and the cascade) for systems learning + medication review going forward.
providerOrders: ADMIT to ortho post-op unit; advance directive 'full code with reassessment if poor functional prognosis' confirmed; daughter (RN background) as POA; geriatric hip fracture multidisciplinary protocol activated

ORTHOPEDIC management: cephalomedullary nail fixation done yesterday (POD 0); WEIGHT-BEARING AS TOLERATED (WBAT) postop; PT consult for mobilization POD 1 (today); OT for ADL retraining; OOB to chair for meals; ambulation with PT assist later today
PHARMACY review — D/C ALL BEERS-LIST MEDS that contributed to fall + delirium risk:
D/C LORAZEPAM 0.5 mg PO BID (the cause of the fall — never restart for anxiety in elderly; alternatives: SSRI like sertraline 25–50 mg low and slow, CBT, social/family engagement)
D/C DIPHENHYDRAMINE PRN sleep (Beers anticholinergic; alternative: sleep hygiene + melatonin 1–3 mg or trazodone 12.5–25 mg if needed)
D/C OXYBUTYNIN PO BID (Beers anticholinergic; alternative: behavioral interventions like scheduled toileting + bladder training, or MIRABEGRON 25 mg PO daily if pharm needed)

D/C AMITRIPTYLINE QHS (Beers anticholinergic + sedating; alternative for neuropathic pain: GABAPENTIN 100 mg PO TID start low + titrate slow, or DULOXETINE 30 mg PO daily, or topical lidocaine 5% patch)
CONTINUE: levothyroxine, amlodipine, atorvastatin, vitamin D, calcium
PAIN MANAGEMENT — multimodal, opioid-sparing, geriatric-safe:
SCHEDULED ACETAMINOPHEN 650 mg PO q6h (NOT PRN — baseline analgesia; safe with normal LFTs and total daily dose <3 g in elderly with frailty/low body weight); check LFTs

HYDROMORPHONE 0.2–0.4 mg IV q4h PRN moderate-severe pain (LOW dose; HYDROMORPHONE preferred over morphine in elderly with renal impairment because no toxic metabolite accumulation; oxycodone PO alternative when tolerating PO)
AVOID MEPERIDINE (Beers — neurotoxic metabolite); AVOID TRAMADOL (lowers seizure threshold, serotonergic interactions, less effective in elderly than thought)
Fascia iliaca block placed preop yesterday; discuss with anesthesia re-dosing OR continuous fascia iliaca catheter for 24–48 hr postop analgesia (excellent opioid-sparing approach for hip fracture)
PAINAD scale q4h to assess pain in confused/delirious patient (PAINAD evaluates breathing, vocalization, facial expression, body language, consolability)
COX-2 selective NSAIDs (celecoxib) generally AVOIDED in elderly with CKD/cardiac disease; topical diclofenac OK for localized pain
DELIRIUM PREVENTION / MANAGEMENT — non-pharmacologic FIRST (HELP bundle):
1:1 sitter; daughter rooming-in (familiar face); bedside large-print clock + calendar visible
Reorientation at every interaction ('Mrs. Lindqvist, you're in the hospital, today is...'); short clear sentences; involve daughter as informational support
Glasses + hearing aids in place at all waking hours (daughter brought from home — KEY intervention); clean aids; insert correctly
Sleep hygiene: lights on day with blinds open / dim and quiet night with NO awakening 2200–0600 unless clinically necessary (sleep is restorative; nighttime awakenings drive delirium)
Mobility OOB to chair for meals; ambulate with PT 3x/day as tolerated (immobility worsens delirium and accelerates functional decline)
Familiar objects at bedside; family photos; favorite music if helpful
AVOID restraints — worsen delirium, increase falls/injuries; bed alarm + low bed + 1:1 sitter instead
Pain control adequate (untreated pain is delirium precipitant)
AVOID Beers meds: NO benzodiazepines (lorazepam, midazolam, alprazolam) unless EtOH/benzo withdrawal; NO diphenhydramine; NO anticholinergics; NO meperidine

Antipsychotics LAST RESORT after non-pharm exhausted + severe agitation threatening safety + informed family discussion (FDA BLACK BOX MORTALITY in dementia patients); if used, HALOPERIDOL 0.25–0.5 mg PO/IM at lowest dose, time-limited, document attempted alternatives

CARDIOPULMONARY: continuous tele monitoring for known paroxysmal AFib; on metoprolol; restart WARFARIN POD 1 (today) — bridge with prophylactic enoxaparin until INR therapeutic (typically POD 4–7); cardiology consult coordinating; ECG q-shift to monitor rhythm

VTE PROPHYLAXIS: pneumatic compression devices (SCDs) bilateral lower extremities CONTINUOUS until ambulating reliably; ENOXAPARIN 40 mg SubQ daily started today (POD 1, 12 hr postop, hemostasis confirmed); continue 28–35 days postop (hip fracture is highest-risk surgery for VTE)

ANEMIA management: Hgb 8.6 borderline cardiac transfusion threshold (FOCUS trial: transfuse if <8 in cardiac patients or symptomatic); trend Hgb q12h initially; transfuse 1 unit PRBC if <8 or symptomatic anemia (dyspnea, chest pain, tachycardia not responsive to pain control); iron studies; iron supplementation for chronic anemia

Foley catheter REMOVAL TODAY (POD 1) per CAUTI prevention protocol; bladder scan post-void if any concern about retention; intermittent straight cath if retention >300 mL; scheduled toileting; external female catheter (PureWick) only if accurate output critical

Bowel care: opioid-induced constipation prevention — scheduled docusate + senna; bowel movement frequency monitoring; rectal exam if no BM x 3 days (constipation is delirium precipitant + can mimic abdominal pathology)

Nutrition: regular diet with cardiac/age-appropriate consistency; oral nutritional supplement (Ensure or similar) between meals (albumin 3.2 = frailty marker; supplementation improves wound healing and recovery); nutrition consult; SLP consult if any swallow concern (delirium can impair swallow)
Skin: pressure injury prevention — daily skin assessment; turn q2h if bed-bound; padded heels; barrier creams; nutrition optimization; mobilization is the best prevention

Sleep hygiene: dim lights 2100, no caffeine after 1400, calm evening routine, NO routine awakening 2200–0600 unless clinically needed; MELATONIN 1–3 mg PO at bedtime PRN (preferred over benzo or diphenhydramine in elderly); LOW-DOSE TRAZODONE 12.5–25 mg if needed (geriatric dosing always start low)

Discharge planning early: case management consult; goal = discharge to skilled nursing facility (SNF) in ~5–7 days for rehabilitation, then home with services if function permits; assessment of home safety (rugs, lighting, grab bars, walker access); medication reconciliation with PERMANENT removal of the Beers-list meds; PCP notified of the lorazepam–fall connection for systems learning

Family meeting with daughter: review trajectory expectations (25% 1-yr mortality post hip fracture; 50% don't return to prior function), delirium recovery timeline (days-weeks in elderly with baseline cognitive impairment), SNF vs home planning, future-fall prevention plan including medication safety advocacy

Outpatient follow-up: ortho clinic in 2 weeks for incision check + hip X-ray; PCP within 1 week (medication reconciliation + delirium follow-up); geriatric clinic referral if not established; physical therapy continued at SNF then home; bone density (DEXA) for osteoporosis treatment evaluation if not done — bisphosphonate or denosumab indicated after hip fracture

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Postoperative delirium CAM POSITIVE (acute change from baseline + inattention + disorganized thinking) — Grade 1 hyperactive in patient with baseline mild cognitive impairment
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — common complication of geriatric hip fracture surgery (25–50% incidence). Risk factors all stacked. Treat with non-pharm bundle first; identify and treat precipitants (pain, infection, medications, hypoxia, dehydration, sleep deprivation). Hyperactive delirium is the visible form; hypoactive is more common AND more missed.

opts.text: LORAZEPAM 0.5 mg PO BID started 10 days ago — the proximate cause of the fall + a major delirium contributor + Beers-list medication absolutely contraindicated in elderly
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the root cause of the admission. The prescribing pattern (new benzo for elderly anxiety) is a sentinel-event-level error. D/C immediately and never restart. The fall-fracture cascade started with this prescribing decision.

opts.text: Polypharmacy with FOUR Beers-list medications (lorazepam, diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, amitriptyline) — high anticholinergic burden + sedating + fall/delirium risk
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — modifiable systemic risk. Beers list 2023 identifies meds with disproportionate harm in elderly. Anticholinergic burden score >3 = high delirium risk. Pharmacy review + deprescribing is the highest-impact intervention.

opts.text: Pain PAINAD 4 at rest / 7 with movement; fascia iliaca block wearing off; untreated pain is a major delirium precipitant
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pain control essential. Scheduled acetaminophen + judicious low-dose hydromorphone PRN; consider fascia iliaca block re-dose or continuous catheter; AVOID meperidine (Beers) + tramadol. Use PAINAD scale (verbal report unreliable in delirium).

opts.text: Hgb 8.6 (down from 11.2 preop) — borderline cardiac transfusion threshold (FOCUS trial: transfuse if Hgb <8 in cardiac patients or symptomatic)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — anemia + cardiac disease + delirium overlap. Trend q12h; transfuse if drops <8 OR symptomatic. Iron studies + supplementation for chronic anemia. Symptomatic threshold higher than the absolute number.

opts.text: Mild hypoxia SpO2 94% on 2 L NC — contributing to delirium; encourage incentive spirometry + early mobilization
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — modifiable delirium contributor. Postop atelectasis common in elderly; treatment is mobilization + IS + adequate pain control (so patient can deep-breathe). Add humidified O2 if needed; target SpO2 ≥92%.

opts.text: Foley catheter still in place POD 1 — CAUTI risk + restraint-like effect + delirium contributor
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — remove today per CAUTI prevention bundle. Bladder scan for retention if any concern; intermittent straight cath or PureWick if accurate output needed; scheduled toileting once removed. Indwelling catheter days = CAUTI risk.

opts.text: AFib on warfarin (held for surgery, partially reversed with 4PCC preop); INR 1.4 — restart warfarin POD 1 with enoxaparin bridge
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — anticoagulation balance. Stroke risk off anticoagulation in elderly AFib is significant. Restart as soon as hemostasis confirmed (typically POD 1); bridge with prophylactic enoxaparin until INR therapeutic. Cardiology + ortho coordination.

opts.text: Hip fracture in elderly = 25% one-year mortality; 50% don't return to prior function — driving comprehensive care + family/goals discussion
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — prognostic context. Early goals-of-care conversation aligns treatment intensity with patient values. Daughter (POA, informed) engaged. Trajectory: SNF rehab → home with services if functional recovery permits.

opts.text: Mild hypoalbuminemia 3.2 (frailty marker) + poor preop nutrition + 25% PO intake today — nutrition optimization needed
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — modifiable factor. Frailty + poor nutrition = worse outcomes (wound healing, infection, recovery). Oral nutritional supplements between meals; nutrition consult; SLP if any swallow concern; encourage family-favored foods.

opts.text: Allergies: sulfa rash
opts.rat: Documented; not relevant to current management. No sulfa antibiotics being given. Document and proceed.

opts.text: Temperature 99.4°F (POD 1 low-grade)
opts.rat: POD 1 low-grade fever is common (atelectasis, inflammatory response) and not necessarily infection. Encourage incentive spirometry + early mobilization + adequate pain control to deep-breathe. Monitor for high fever (>38.5°C) or sustained fever which would prompt infection workup.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What is happening physiologically and pharmacologically?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles of geriatric care.

opts.text: Hip fracture in elderly = 25% ONE-YEAR MORTALITY; ~50% of survivors don't return to prior function; SURGERY WITHIN 24–48 HOURS reduces mortality, pneumonia, delirium, DVT, decubitus injury compared to delayed surgery (multiple geriatric hip fracture pathway trials)
opts.rat: CORRECT — defining epidemiology of geriatric hip fracture. Early surgery + multidisciplinary care + delirium prevention + early mobilization + DVT prophylaxis + nutrition optimization = best outcomes. The 'geriatric hip fracture' pathway is one of the most studied and standardized care bundles.

opts.text: BEERS LIST (American Geriatrics Society 2023) identifies medications with disproportionate harm in elderly. Key categories: BENZODIAZEPINES (any — fall + delirium risk), ANTICHOLINERGICS (diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, TCAs, certain antiemetics), Z-DRUGS (zolpidem), ANTIPSYCHOTICS in dementia (FDA black-box mortality), PPIs long-term, NSAIDs in CKD, sliding-scale insulin alone, MEPERIDINE (neurotoxic metabolite), TRAMADOL (seizure, serotonin)
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield. Anticholinergic burden is cumulative — patients on 3+ anticholinergics have markedly higher delirium and fall risk. This patient was on 4 Beers meds simultaneously. Deprescribing is the single highest-impact intervention.

opts.text: CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) requires: (1) acute onset + fluctuating course, (2) inattention, AND either (3) disorganized thinking OR (4) altered LOC. Sensitive screening tool. Hyperactive delirium = agitation/restlessness (visible); HYPOACTIVE = withdrawn/quiet (MORE COMMON and MORE MISSED; higher mortality)
opts.rat: CORRECT — diagnostic standard. Screen all elderly inpatients q-shift. Hypoactive delirium often missed because patients are 'quiet' — but mortality is higher. CAM-ICU adapted version for intubated patients.

opts.text: Non-pharmacologic delirium management (HELP — Hospital Elder Life Program) is FIRST-LINE: reorientation, glasses/hearing aids, sleep-wake cycle, mobility, hydration, familiar objects, family presence, pain control. Reduces delirium incidence by ~30–40% in trials
opts.rat: CORRECT — evidence-based bundle. Each element addresses a precipitant. The sensory restoration (glasses + hearing aids) is the most-often-missed simple intervention. Pharmacology (antipsychotics) does NOT shorten delirium and carries dementia mortality risk — last resort only.

opts.text: WEIGHT-BEARING AS TOLERATED (WBAT) is the modern standard postop for most geriatric hip fracture fixations; the older 'NWB x 6 weeks' is outdated and harmful (immobility worsens delirium, pneumonia, DVT, decubitus, functional decline, mortality more than the marginal benefit of strict NWB)
opts.rat: CORRECT — practice change from historical teaching. Modern fixation hardware (cephalomedullary nails, dynamic hip screws, arthroplasties) tolerates immediate weight bearing. PT POD 1 is standard. Immobility kills more elderly than micro-implant displacement.

opts.text: VTE prophylaxis for hip fracture surgery: SCDs bilateral CONTINUOUS + pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH 40 mg SubQ daily, fondaparinux 2.5 mg SubQ daily, DOAC, or warfarin) for 28–35 DAYS postop; hip fracture is HIGHEST-RISK surgery for VTE (DVT 40–60%, PE 5–10% without prophylaxis)
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield. Pharm prophylaxis usually starts 12–24 hr postop after hemostasis confirmed. Aspirin alone is option for low-risk per ACCP but most hip fractures get LMWH or DOAC. Bleeding risk balanced against high VTE risk.

opts.text: Cardiac transfusion threshold in elderly with cardiac disease: Hgb <8 g/dL (FOCUS trial, NEJM 2011); transfuse if Hgb <8 OR symptomatic anemia; restrictive strategy reduces complications vs liberal threshold of Hgb <10
opts.rat: CORRECT — important threshold. FOCUS trial showed restrictive transfusion (<8) in hip fracture patients with cardiac disease equivalent or better outcomes than liberal (<10). Trend Hgb; transfuse based on threshold + symptoms; not 'just one unit for low Hgb.'

opts.text: Pain management in geriatric postop: SCHEDULED acetaminophen + low-dose opioid PRN + regional anesthesia (fascia iliaca block) = opioid-sparing multimodal approach; AVOID meperidine (Beers — neurotoxic metabolite), AVOID tramadol (Beers — serotonin/seizure), AVOID benzo for muscle spasm, USE hydromorphone over morphine in elderly with renal impairment
opts.rat: CORRECT — multimodal pain management. Scheduled tylenol = baseline analgesia; opioid only for breakthrough at low dose. Fascia iliaca block + continuous catheter is excellent for hip fracture. PAINAD scale for nonverbal/confused patients.

opts.text: Diphenhydramine 25 mg PO at bedtime is the appropriate sleep aid for this 84-year-old with postop sleep disturbance
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS — Beers list anticholinergic; worsens cognition, causes urinary retention, falls, delirium. NEVER use diphenhydramine for sleep in elderly. Alternatives: non-pharm sleep hygiene first; melatonin 1–3 mg; low-dose trazodone 12.5–25 mg if needed.

opts.text: Lorazepam 0.5 mg IV PRN agitation is appropriate first-line for the postop delirium
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Benzodiazepines WORSEN delirium (paradoxical effect; disinhibition; sedation that mimics improvement but is actually worsening). Reserve benzos for EtOH/benzo withdrawal only. Non-pharm bundle first; if absolutely necessary for severe agitation threatening safety, LOW-DOSE haloperidol 0.25–0.5 mg PO/IM (not first-line; with informed family discussion given black-box dementia mortality).

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Postoperative Delirium r/t multiple precipitants (surgery, pain, opioid, anesthesia, Beers meds, hypoxia, sleep deprivation, foley, unfamiliar environment, baseline cognitive impairment); reversible if precipitants addressed
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Delirium is the most common postop complication in elderly hip fracture; raises mortality + length of stay + SNF discharge rates. Reversible if precipitants addressed. Non-pharm bundle + Beers med D/C + pain control + mobilization + sensory restoration.

opts.text: Risk for Future Falls / Re-injury r/t polypharmacy with multiple Beers meds + baseline mobility decline + recent fracture + delirium + sensory impairment
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Without addressing the root causes (Beers meds, environment, mobility), the next fall is coming. Pharmacy review + deprescribing + home safety assessment + PT/OT + walker reassessment + future-prescription advocacy + family awareness.

opts.text: Acute Pain r/t hip fracture and surgery; PAINAD 4 at rest / 7 with movement; untreated pain is a major delirium precipitant
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Scheduled acetaminophen + judicious low-dose hydromorphone PRN + fascia iliaca block re-dose or continuous catheter. AVOID meperidine, tramadol, benzo. PAINAD scale q4h for nonverbal/confused patients. Adequate pain control is essential for mobilization.

opts.text: Risk for VTE / PE r/t hip fracture (highest-risk surgery) + immobility + age + AFib history
opts.rat: #4 — Significant but actively addressed. SCDs continuous + enoxaparin 40 mg SubQ daily started POD 1 + continue 28–35 days. Mobilization reduces VTE risk further. Restart warfarin POD 1 with enoxaparin bridge given AFib history.

opts.text: Risk for Decreased Mobility / Functional Decline r/t hip fracture + delirium + pain + age + frailty; ~50% don't return to prior function
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Early mobilization POD 1 (WBAT); PT/OT consult; multidisciplinary discharge planning to SNF for rehab; nutrition optimization; goal-setting with daughter + patient when delirium clears.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: D/C all Beers-list medications: lorazepam, diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, amitriptyline; replace with non-Beers alternatives or non-pharm approaches
opts.rat: Single highest-impact intervention. Each Beers med independently raises fall + delirium risk; together they form a cascade. Pharmacy review + permanent removal. PCP notified for systems learning to avoid restarting.

opts.text: Non-pharm delirium bundle (HELP): 1:1 sitter, daughter rooming-in, large-print clock + calendar, glasses + hearing aids, reorientation, sleep hygiene (no awakenings 2200–0600), mobility, minimize stimulation, familiar objects
opts.rat: First-line and most effective. Evidence-based delirium reduction. Glasses + hearing aids (daughter brought from home) = KEY simple intervention. Document interventions; train all staff to engage in reorientation.

opts.text: Lorazepam 0.5 mg IV PRN agitation for the postop delirium
opts.rat: ⚠️ Benzodiazepines WORSEN delirium. Beers list. The lorazepam was the CAUSE of the original fall. Never give to this patient. If pharmacology needed for severe agitation threatening safety, low-dose haloperidol 0.25–0.5 mg with informed discussion (black-box dementia mortality).

opts.text: Diphenhydramine 25 mg PO at bedtime for sleep
opts.rat: ⚠️ Beers anticholinergic — worsens delirium, urinary retention, falls. Alternatives: sleep hygiene first; melatonin 1–3 mg; low-dose trazodone 12.5–25 mg if needed. Never use diphenhydramine for sleep in elderly.

opts.text: Scheduled acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h + hydromorphone 0.2 mg IV q4h PRN moderate-severe pain; fascia iliaca block re-dose or continuous catheter; PAINAD scale q4h
opts.rat: Multimodal opioid-sparing approach. Scheduled tylenol = baseline; opioid for breakthrough at low dose; hydromorphone safer than morphine in elderly with CKD; fascia iliaca block excellent. PAINAD for nonverbal/confused.

opts.text: Meperidine 50 mg IM q3h PRN for pain
opts.rat: ⚠️ Beers list — neurotoxic metabolite (normeperidine) accumulates in elderly with renal impairment causing tremor, seizure, delirium. NEVER use meperidine in elderly. Hydromorphone or oxycodone at low dose preferred.

opts.text: REMOVE foley catheter TODAY (POD 1) per CAUTI prevention; bladder scan if any concern about retention; scheduled toileting; intermittent straight cath if retention >300 mL
opts.rat: CAUTI prevention bundle. Foley indications narrow (retention, accurate UOP for hemodynamic instability, end-of-life comfort, large stage IV decubitus). Postop foley should come out within 24 hr if no indication. Restraint-like effect impedes mobilization.

opts.text: WBAT (weight-bearing as tolerated) postop; PT/OT POD 1 with OOB to chair + ambulation as tolerated; advance daily
opts.rat: Modern standard for geriatric hip fracture. Early mobilization reduces delirium, pneumonia, DVT, decubitus, functional decline, mortality. Outdated NWB practice causes more harm than benefit for typical fixations.

opts.text: Strict bed rest x 6 weeks postop to prevent implant displacement
opts.rat: ⚠️ Outdated practice. Strict NWB causes more harm than the marginal benefit of avoiding micro-implant displacement. Modern fixation tolerates WBAT. Immobility = delirium, pneumonia, DVT, decubitus, functional decline, mortality.

opts.text: VTE prophylaxis: SCDs bilateral continuous + enoxaparin 40 mg SubQ daily for 28–35 days postop; warfarin restart POD 1 with enoxaparin bridge until INR therapeutic (AFib history)
opts.rat: Highest-risk surgery for VTE. Multimodal (mechanical + pharm). Duration extended (4–5 weeks). AFib history requires anticoagulation continuation — restart warfarin POD 1 with bridge. Watch for bleeding.

opts.text: Trend Hgb q12h; transfuse 1 unit PRBC if <8 in this cardiac patient OR symptomatic anemia (FOCUS trial threshold)
opts.rat: Restrictive transfusion strategy. Trend; transfuse based on threshold + symptoms. Iron studies; iron supplementation for chronic anemia. Avoid liberal transfusion (no benefit, transfusion risks).

opts.text: Discharge planning with case management: SNF rehab in 5–7 days; home safety assessment; medication reconciliation with permanent Beers-meds removal; PCP notification of fall–medication connection; family meeting; outpatient follow-up structure
opts.rat: Multidisciplinary discharge planning starts early. SNF for short-term rehab + safe transition. Home modifications (grab bars, rugs, lighting, walker access). Medication review at every encounter going forward. Geriatric clinic referral if not established.

opts.text: Bone density (DEXA) for osteoporosis evaluation; bisphosphonate or denosumab after hip fracture (secondary fracture prevention)
opts.rat: Post-hip-fracture care includes osteoporosis evaluation and treatment. Hip fracture itself diagnoses osteoporosis (regardless of T-score). DEXA + treatment with bisphosphonate (alendronate, zoledronic acid) or denosumab; vitamin D + calcium; fall-prevention program.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm pharmacy D/C of all Beers-list meds (lorazepam, diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, amitriptyline); flag chart 'NO BENZODIAZEPINES, NO DIPHENHYDRAMINE, NO ANTICHOLINERGICS'; document teach-back with daughter
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Stop the harm; prevent future error. Chart alert prevents wrong-prescriber restart. Daughter as informed advocate going forward. Each missed dose is a step toward delirium resolution.

opts.text: Non-pharm delirium bundle: 1:1 sitter, daughter rooming-in, glasses + hearing aids in place, bedside large-print clock, reorientation at every interaction, scheduled toileting, sleep hygiene plan
opts.rat: #2 — Concurrent with #1. Evidence-based delirium reduction. Glasses + hearing aids = the most-overlooked simple intervention. Daughter at bedside = familiar face. Train staff for consistent reorientation.

opts.text: Adequate pain control: scheduled acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h + hydromorphone 0.2 mg IV q4h PRN; PAINAD scale q4h; discuss fascia iliaca block re-dose or continuous catheter with anesthesia; AVOID meperidine, tramadol, benzo, gabapentin high dose
opts.rat: #3 — Untreated pain drives delirium and limits mobilization. Multimodal opioid-sparing. PAINAD for the confused/delirious patient. Adequate control enables mobility + sleep + reduced opioid exposure.

opts.text: Remove foley TODAY (POD 1); PT to OOB to chair for breakfast then ambulation later today; enoxaparin 40 mg SubQ given POD 1; SCDs continuous; warfarin restart with bridge; trend Hgb q12h
opts.rat: #4 — Multi-system early postop care. Foley removal reduces CAUTI + restraint effect. Mobilization is delirium therapy + VTE prevention + pneumonia prevention + functional preservation. VTE prophylaxis essential. Hgb trending.

opts.text: Discharge planning: case management + SNF placement in 5–7 days + medication reconciliation with permanent Beers-meds removal + PCP notification of fall–medication connection + family meeting + home safety assessment + outpatient follow-up + DEXA + osteoporosis treatment
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained care planning. SNF for short-term rehab. Medication review at every future encounter. Home modifications + PT/OT + fall prevention + osteoporosis treatment + future-prescription advocacy by daughter.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: POD 2: CAM negative this AM; oriented x3; pain 3/10 controlled with scheduled tylenol + 1 prn hydromorphone; OOB to chair for all meals; ambulated 25 feet with walker + PT; Hgb 8.4 stable
opts.rat: Excellent trajectory. Delirium resolution as precipitants addressed. Mobilization on track. Pain controlled. Hgb stable above transfusion threshold. Continue current plan; advance mobility daily; case management for SNF placement.

opts.text: Discharge day 5 to SNF: ambulating 100 feet with walker + 1-person assist; CAM negative consistently x 48 hr; medication reconciliation completed with PERMANENT removal of Beers meds; daughter has written advocacy plan; PCP notified of cascade; outpatient orthopedic + PCP + geriatric clinic appointments scheduled; DEXA + osteoporosis treatment plan in SNF
opts.rat: Successful transition. SNF for ongoing rehab; goal home with services in 3–4 weeks if function permits. Daughter empowered to advocate for medication safety. Permanent Beers-meds removal reduces future cascade risk.

opts.text: 3-month follow-up at PCP: living at home with daily check-ins; ambulating with cane indoors, walker outdoors; baseline cognitive function with MMSE 24 (matches her pre-fall baseline); no medication changes; PCP verbalizes ongoing avoidance of Beers meds; doing OT exercises; community fall-prevention program engaged
opts.rat: Best-case outcome — returned to baseline function. The post-hip-fracture trajectory was favorable due to early surgery, aggressive multidisciplinary care, polypharmacy correction, and engaged family. Ongoing prevention focus.

opts.text: POD 2: cross-cover night provider orders lorazepam 0.5 mg PO PRN agitation; nurse questions order citing chart alert; provider refuses to cancel; nurse escalates to charge RN and pharmacy; order is held pending attending review; family informed
opts.rat: System safety win. The chart alert + nurse vigilance + escalation prevented the second iatrogenic Beers-med exposure. RN has authority to hold a medication pending clarification when there is a clinical safety concern. Document escalation; system learning for cross-cover providers.

opts.text: POD 3: Hgb drops to 7.4 with dyspnea and tachycardia HR 118; transfused 1 unit PRBC per FOCUS threshold; symptoms improve; Hgb stabilizes at 8.6 post-transfusion
opts.rat: Appropriate transfusion per FOCUS trial threshold (Hgb <8 OR symptomatic). Symptoms guide more than the absolute number. Restrictive strategy maintained. Iron studies + supplementation for ongoing recovery.

opts.text: POD 4: develops dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, mild hypoxia SpO2 90% on 2 L NC; D-dimer elevated; CT-PA shows segmental PE — VTE despite prophylaxis (breakthrough)
opts.rat: PE despite prophylaxis (5–10% of hip fracture VTE despite prophylaxis). Confirm with CT-PA; therapeutic anticoagulation (heparin gtt or therapeutic-dose enoxaparin); continue mobilization once stable; ICU consideration if hemodynamically unstable. Risk of bleeding balanced against need for treatment.

opts.text: POD 6: still confused (hypoactive — withdrawn, oriented to self only, not eating, refusing PT); CAM positive intermittently; persistent delirium despite non-pharm bundle
opts.rat: Persistent delirium is common in elderly with baseline cognitive impairment (weeks-to-months to fully resolve). Re-evaluate precipitants: hypoxia, infection (recheck), constipation, pain control, medication review, sleep, hydration. Continue non-pharm bundle; avoid Beers meds; consider depression contribution; psychiatry consult.

opts.text: After moving to SNF, the SNF physician restarts her on amitriptyline 25 mg QHS because 'she's been complaining of neuropathy in her foot'; daughter intervenes and shows the written Beers-meds list; SNF MD recants and switches to gabapentin 100 mg PO TID
opts.rat: System safety success — daughter as informed advocate. The written list traveled to the SNF with the discharge plan and prevented the cascade restart. Daughter empowerment was the key intervention. Reinforce ongoing advocacy at every provider visit.

opts.text: Persistent mild bilateral lower extremity edema POD 7; no new pain or warmth; lungs clear; cardiac stable; UOP normal
opts.rat: Expected post-surgery + bedrest residual. Continue elevation, SCDs, mobilization, gradual diuresis if continues. Distinguish from new DVT (asymmetric, warm, tender — would warrant ultrasound) or HF exacerbation (lung findings).

opts.text: POD 8 family meeting: daughter satisfied with trajectory; verbalizes she will advocate for medication safety at every future encounter; signs medication reconciliation; outpatient follow-up appointments confirmed; SNF discharge tomorrow
opts.rat: Successful transition. Daughter empowered with knowledge and written documentation. Medication reconciliation completed and reinforced. Outpatient structure in place. SNF rehab next phase; home with services target.
